stem: In a school performance study comparing School A vs. School B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher test score improvement (73.1% vs 58.2%). However, when stratified by socioeconomic background: In affluent area students (n_X=30, n_Y=120): Treatment X achieves 90% vs Treatment Y's 84%. In lower-income area students (n_X=80, n_Y=40): Treatment X achieves 46% vs Treatment Y's 41%. The true population proportion of affluent area students is uncertain, estimated between 39% and 54%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to lower-income area students (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.054, 0.056], point estimate: 0.055. Treatment X is genuinely better by 5.5 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=58.2%, Y=73.1% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In affluent area students: X=90% > Y=84%. In lower-income area students: X=46% > Y=41%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because School A is applied more to lower-income area students (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
